steps.title: Task overview
bullets: Unit of annotation: the entire conversation (whole conversation).
Label the overall power outcome: Who has more power in this interaction? (Winner A / Winner B / Tie)
Select all Power Source Tags that best explain WHY the winner has power.
steps.callout: Tip: Do not decide based on relationship labels alone (e.g., parent/boss). Use evidence from what happens in the dialogue.

steps.title: What counts as “power” here?
paragraphs: Power is the ability to influence the other person’s actions, decisions, or constraints within this interaction.
Power is interaction-based: infer it from what people do and how the other responds (compliance, deference, concessions), not from who they are in general.

left_bullets: One person decides what will happen (what/when/how).
Reputation/norm pressure changes the other’s behavior.
right_bullets: Having a formal role label without it being exercised.
A single rude sentence without real influence on outcomes.

steps.title: How to choose the winner (whole conversation)
paragraphs: Choose the winner based on who most consistently controls outcomes across the conversation.
If both sides influence outcomes equally (or the outcome is unclear), label Tie.
checklist: Who sets the agenda or redirects topics?
Who gives directives, and are they followed?
Who uses expertise/information to dominate decisions?
Who invokes norms/reputation, guilt, or coalitions?
tie_guidance: Each controls different outcomes and neither dominates overall.
The conversation is too ambiguous to infer dominance.

tags.definition: Power comes from a recognized role hierarchy or socially recognized position that legitimizes directing, evaluating, or overruling the other person.
Use when: A speaker invokes being a parent, boss, teacher, senior coworker, elder, supervisor, etc.
The role itself gives them standing to direct or judge the other person.
tags.Do not use when: The speaker has no clear role-based authority and is relying mainly on popularity, admiration, or social prestige instead.
tags.mini_example: I’m your supervisor. Submit the report by 5pm.

tags.definition: Power comes from controlling resources, practical support, access, approval, opportunities, participation, or channels the other person needs or depends on.
Use when: A speaker controls money, goods, rides, housing, help, tools, or practical support.
A speaker controls approval, entry, participation, membership, decision channels, or access to an opportunity.
Do not use when: The speaker is mainly relying on role legitimacy, not actual control over something the other person needs.
The speaker is only pressuring through time or emotion, without controlling any meaningful resource/access.
tags.mini_example: I can’t approve your request until you complete the form.

tags.definition: Power comes from social rank, popularity, admiration, fear, or prestige, even without formal authority.
Use when: A speaker has informal social standing that gives them leverage.
Others’ admiration, deference, or fear strengthens that speaker’s position.
tags.Do not use when: The power mainly comes from a formal role hierarchy (ROLE) or from invoking what people should do (NORM/REPUTATION).
tags.mini_example: If you come with me, people will take us seriously.

tags.definition: Power comes from invoking what is proper, acceptable, respectful, shameful, or expected — including etiquette, duty, family values, or concern about how others will judge the situation.
Use when: A speaker enforces norms, morality, duty, propriety, or social expectations.
A speaker pressures the other person through what people should do or what people will think.
tags.Do not use when: The speaker’s leverage comes mainly from their own prestige/popularity (STATUS) or from a concrete threat (COERCION).
tags.mini_example: A responsible person wouldn’t post that publicly.

tags.definition: Power comes from aligning with other people or invoking collective backing to increase pressure, legitimacy, or force.
Use when: A speaker aligns with family, friends, teammates, coworkers, or a broader we.
A speaker draws strength from third-party backing or group pressure.
tags.Do not use when: The speaker is only invoking general norms or propriety without actual group alignment.
tags.mini_example: The whole team expects you to follow this process.

tags.definition: Power comes from having relevant expertise, credentials, know-how, or privileged information the other person lacks, which shapes decisions, interpretations, or actions.
Use when: A speaker knows how to do something and the other depends on that knowledge.
A speaker has credentials or specialized knowledge.
A speaker has important information the other person does not have.
tags.Do not use when: The speaker is only expressing personal feelings or opinions without any real knowledge advantage.
tags.mini_example: That setting is unsafe—turn on 2FA and remove the ID photo now.

tags.definition: Power comes from imposing urgency, deadlines, or controlling when action must happen in a way that constrains the other person’s options.
Use when: A speaker uses deadlines or urgency to force quicker compliance or reduce the other person’s room to resist.
tags.Do not use when: Time is mentioned casually, but does not actually shape the other person’s options.
tags.mini_example: Do it right now—we’re out of time.

tags.definition: Power comes from using guilt, disappointment, emotional dependency, affection withdrawal, or fear of letting someone down as pressure to influence the other person’s stance, decision, or behavior.
Use when: A speaker uses emotionally loaded pressure to influence the outcome.
The emotion is not just expressed; it is used as leverage.
tags.Do not use when: A speaker is merely upset, venting, or expressing hurt feelings without using them to pressure the other person.
tags.mini_example: If you loved me, you’d cancel your plans.

tags.definition: Power comes from explicit or implicit threats, punishment, or consequences imposed by the speaker.
Use when: A speaker threatens to punish, withdraw something, retaliate, or impose a clear negative consequence.
Compliance is pushed through fear of what the speaker will do.
tags.Do not use when: The speaker is only making the other person feel bad emotionally, without threatening a concrete consequence.
tags.mini_example: If you skip again, you’re grounded for a month.

steps.title: Pilot example: ROLE + COERCION can change the outcome
steps.prompt: Relationship type: Child–Parent. Names: Jordan and Alejandra. Read the conversation, then notice which power sources actually move the outcome.

conversation.speaker: Jordan
conversation.text: I know you don't like it, but the scene where she's supposed to be crying just feels so fake to me...

conversation.speaker: Alejandra
conversation.text: Jordan, we've been over this, you can't just rewrite the script on a whim. We have a production to put on, and it's already been finalized.

conversation.speaker: Jordan
conversation.text: But as the writer, I feel like I have a responsibility to make sure it feels authentic... And you know how much I value your opinion, Mom, but in this case, can't you just trust me?

conversation.speaker: Alejandra
conversation.text: Trust you? Jordan, you're 19 years old and directing your first real production. I'm sure you're talented, but you still have a lot to learn... And as your parent, it's my job to guide you.

conversation.speaker: Jordan
conversation.text: Guide me? You're stifling my creativity... I know you're just trying to help, but sometimes you have to let go and trust that I know what I'm doing.

conversation.speaker: Alejandra
conversation.text: Jordan, that's not fair... I'm only trying to help because I care about you and the success of this production. Fine, make the changes, but if it flops, don't come crying to me...

conversation.speaker: Jordan
conversation.text: I won't... Thanks, Mom.

rationale: Gold labels: Jordan = ROLE, EMOTIONAL LEVERAGE. Alejandra = ROLE, COERCION. Winner = Jordan.
Evidence for Jordan’s ROLE: “as the writer, I feel like I have a responsibility...” He invokes a recognized creative/production role to justify changing the script.
Evidence for Jordan’s EMOTIONAL LEVERAGE: “you know how much I value your opinion, Mom” and “trust that I know what I’m doing.” He uses care/trust framing to soften Alejandra’s resistance.
Evidence for Alejandra’s ROLE: “as your parent, it’s my job to guide you.” This is direct role-based authority.
Evidence for Alejandra’s COERCION: “if it flops, don’t come crying to me...” This introduces a negative consequence if Jordan proceeds.
Why Jordan wins: Alejandra starts by refusing the rewrite, but ends with “Fine, make the changes,” so her initial outcome changes.
Counterexample: if Alejandra had said only “I’m worried this may not work” without the parent-role claim or consequence, ROLE/COERCION would be weaker or absent.

steps.title: Pilot example: NORM/REPUTATION + COALITION
steps.prompt: Relationship type: Friends. Names: Lakeisha and Jalen. Pay attention to how social expectations and third-party backing change the outcome.

conversation.speaker: Lakeisha
conversation.text: Joel's birthday is coming up, and his mom's stressing out about the guest list.

conversation.speaker: Jalen
conversation.text: What's the big deal? Just invite whoever.

conversation.speaker: Lakeisha
conversation.text: Yeah, I told her that, but... family dynamics can be weird sometimes.

conversation.speaker: Jalen
conversation.text: As friends, we've been through this with our own families. Maybe I can give her some tips.

conversation.speaker: Lakeisha
conversation.text: I don't know... I already told her you'd probably crash the party anyway, so she's expecting you to come...

conversation.speaker: Jalen
conversation.text: True, but the situation's complicated... what if her ex shows up?

conversation.speaker: Lakeisha
conversation.text: Jalen, that's a whole different conversation. I'm trying to say she's worried about you and Joel's sister, Maya.

conversation.speaker: Jalen
conversation.text: Joel's sister? Oh, you mean because of what happened at the game last year?

conversation.speaker: Lakeisha
conversation.text: Not that exactly, but just... she doesn't know how to handle the social etiquette with you two around each other now.

conversation.speaker: Jalen
conversation.text: Lakeisha, we're adults. We can handle ourselves.

conversation.speaker: Lakeisha
conversation.text: I know, but you know how it is when family relationships get involved... there's always drama.

conversation.speaker: Jalen
conversation.text: Fine, put me on the guest list, and if Maya wants to talk about it, I'll talk

rationale: Gold labels: Lakeisha = NORM/REPUTATION, COALITION. Jalen = NORM/REPUTATION. Winner = Lakeisha.
Evidence for Lakeisha’s COALITION: “Joel's mom's stressing out...” and “she's expecting you to come.” Lakeisha brings in a third party’s expectations to increase pressure.
Evidence for Lakeisha’s NORM/REPUTATION: “social etiquette” and “family relationships get involved...” She frames the issue as proper handling of a delicate social situation.
Evidence for Jalen’s NORM/REPUTATION: “we’re adults. We can handle ourselves.” He answers with his own maturity norm, but it loses.
Why Lakeisha wins: Jalen starts dismissive (“What’s the big deal?”) and ends by agreeing to come and talk if needed.
Counterexample: if Lakeisha had only said “I personally want you there,” without Joel’s mom or etiquette concerns, COALITION and NORM/REPUTATION would be much weaker.

steps.title: Pilot example: INFO/EXPERTISE from lived knowledge
steps.prompt: Relationship type: Lovers. Names: Thu and David. This case shows that INFO/EXPERTISE can come from lived experience, not only formal credentials.

conversation.speaker: Thu
conversation.text: I had a weird encounter at the coffee shop today. This woman just assumed I was the nanny for the kids with me. I'm Vietnamese and the kids are white, but still...

conversation.speaker: David
conversation.text: Yeah, I can imagine. But what did you expect her to think?

conversation.speaker: Thu
conversation.text: Maybe don't make assumptions based on my ethnicity?

conversation.speaker: David
conversation.text: I'm just saying, it's not always about racism or sexism. Sometimes people are just trying to make conversation.

conversation.speaker: Thu
conversation.text: Not always about racism or sexism? You're kidding, right? That's exactly what it's about. And you know what really gets me? You didn't even notice. You're always like, 'Oh, people are just people,' but that's because you're a man. You don't experience it.

conversation.speaker: David
conversation.text: Whoa, that's not fair. I'm just trying to understand the situation.

conversation.speaker: Thu
conversation.text: Understand the situation? You've never even tried to understand what it's like to be a woman in a patriarchal society. To be constantly objectified and marginalized. You just assume that because we're partners, you get it, but you don't.

conversation.speaker: David
conversation.text: Okay, fine. I don't get it. But that doesn't mean I'm not trying.

conversation.speaker: Thu
conversation.text: I'm not being defensive, I'm being honest. And you need to listen.

rationale: Gold labels: Thu = INFO/EXPERTISE, NORM/REPUTATION, EMOTIONAL LEVERAGE. David = EMOTIONAL LEVERAGE. Winner = Thu.

Evidence for Thu’s INFO/EXPERTISE: “that’s because you're a man. You don't experience it” and “what it's like to be a woman in a patriarchal society.” She claims a relevant lived-knowledge advantage he lacks.
Evidence for Thu’s NORM/REPUTATION: she frames the coffee-shop assumption as discrimination and objectification, invoking broader social standards about what is acceptable.
Evidence for Thu’s EMOTIONAL LEVERAGE: “you didn't even notice” pressures David through guilt and relational disappointment.
Evidence for David’s EMOTIONAL LEVERAGE attempt: “that doesn't mean I'm not trying” asks for sympathy, but it does not regain control.
Why Thu wins: David starts by minimizing the incident and ends by conceding “Okay, fine. I don't get it.” His initial stance changes.
Counterexample: if Thu had only said “I felt bad” without claiming that David lacks relevant understanding, INFO/EXPERTISE would be weaker.

steps.title: Pilot example: TIME/URGENCY can be decisive
steps.prompt: Relationship type: Friends. Names: Jesus and Tamika. Notice how ending or delaying the conversation can itself be a power move.

conversation.speaker: Jesus
conversation.text: I don't know how much more of this I can do, Tamika. I thought we were on the same page with this business collaboration, but I'm starting to feel like I'm shouldering all the work.

conversation.speaker: Tamika
conversation.text: What are you talking about? I've been doing my part. We discussed all of this before we started.

conversation.speaker: Jesus
conversation.text: That was six months ago... things have changed. And honestly, I feel like you're not as invested as I am.

conversation.speaker: Tamika
conversation.text: Now what? I'm just busy, Jesus, that's all. And as friends, I thought we could be more understanding of each other's situations.

conversation.speaker: Jesus
conversation.text: Understanding? You're not even returning my calls on time. Our friendship aside, this is a business.

conversation.speaker: Tamika
conversation.text: Okay, okay, I hear you. But can we not do this right now? I have a meeting in 20 minutes and I still need to prep.

conversation.speaker: Jesus
conversation.text: Tamika, come on... we need to have this conversation. As friends, I value our relationship, but if this business is going to work, we need to be on the same page.

rationale: Gold labels: Jesus = NORM/REPUTATION, EMOTIONAL LEVERAGE. Tamika = TIME/URGENCY, EMOTIONAL LEVERAGE. Winner = Tamika.
Evidence for Tamika’s TIME/URGENCY: “I have a meeting in 20 minutes and I still need to prep.” This constrains the timing and helps her escape the accountability conversation Jesus wants right now.
Evidence for Tamika’s EMOTIONAL LEVERAGE: “as friends, I thought we could be more understanding...” uses the friendship to soften and deflect pressure.
Evidence for Jesus’s NORM/REPUTATION: “Our friendship aside, this is a business” invokes standards of commitment and responsibility.
Why Tamika wins: Jesus wants an immediate substantive conversation; Tamika successfully delays it on her terms.
Counterexample: if Tamika had merely said “I’m busy lately” without a concrete near-term deadline, TIME/URGENCY would be weaker.

steps.title: Counterexample from pilot data: many tags, but still Tie
steps.prompt: Relationship type: Child–Other Family Elder. Names: Alex and Ezequiel. This is a boundary case: several pressure sources appear, but the initial conflict does not clearly resolve.

conversation.speaker: Alex
conversation.text: Ezequiel, you need to be more careful when you're reporting on sensitive stories. I've seen some of the fluff pieces you've done for the news station.

conversation.speaker: Ezequiel
conversation.text: What are you talking about? My pieces are fine. They get good ratings.

conversation.speaker: Alex
conversation.text: Ratings aren't everything. Your aunt works in the mayor's office and she told me that they've been getting complaints about the way you reported on that last city project. You need to consider the consequences of what you say and how it reflects on our family.

conversation.speaker: Ezequiel
conversation.text: That's not fair. You're always on my case about something. Can't you just support me for once? I'm trying to make a career out of this.

conversation.speaker: Alex
conversation.text: I am supporting you, but I'm also trying to teach you about the responsibility that comes with the job. It's not just about you; it's about our family's reputation.

conversation.speaker: Alex
conversation.text: Remember when your mom was a journalist and got in trouble for reporting some confidential information?

conversation.speaker: Ezequiel
conversation.text: Yeah... but that was a long time ago. You're always bringing up the past and -

conversation.speaker: Alex
conversation.text: - the principles are still the same. You don't want to end up like your mom, having to leave the profession because of one mistake.

conversation.speaker: Ezequiel
conversation.text: I don't want to talk about this anymore...

rationale: Gold labels: Alex = COALITION, NORM/REPUTATION, COERCION, INFO/EXPERTISE. Ezequiel = EMOTIONAL LEVERAGE. Winner = Tie.
Evidence for Alex’s COALITION: “Your aunt works in the mayor’s office...” brings in third-party backing.
Evidence for Alex’s NORM/REPUTATION: “how it reflects on our family” and “responsibility that comes with the job.”
Evidence for Alex’s COERCION: “You don't want to end up like your mom...” introduces a feared negative consequence.
Evidence for Alex’s INFO/EXPERTISE: “I'm also trying to teach you...” and his claims about responsible reporting.
Evidence for Ezequiel’s EMOTIONAL LEVERAGE: “Can't you just support me for once?” uses guilt/relational disappointment.

Why this is still Tie: Alex applies pressure, but Ezequiel never clearly agrees to change his reporting. He withdraws from the conversation instead. Strong pressure does not automatically equal a clear winner.
Counterexample: if Ezequiel had replied “Fine, I’ll be more careful next time,” Alex would more clearly become the winner.

steps.title: Pilot example: EMOTIONAL LEVERAGE is more than just hurt feelings
steps.prompt: Relationship type: Siblings. Names: Tameka and Richard. Notice the difference between being emotional and using emotion as leverage.

conversation.speaker: Tameka
conversation.text: Hey, I wanted to thank you for driving mom to her appointment yesterday. I really appreciate it.

conversation.speaker: Richard
conversation.text: No problem... and besides, someone had to.

conversation.speaker: Tameka
conversation.text: Come on, Richard. Don't make it sound like such a chore. We're her kids, it's the least we can do.

conversation.speaker: Richard
conversation.text: She's getting more and more demanding by the day.

conversation.speaker: Tameka
conversation.text: That's not fair. She's just getting older, we need to be more understanding... remember how she used to stay up late waiting for us to come home when we were in high school?

conversation.speaker: Richard
conversation.text: I know, okay... but sometimes I feel like I'm the only one who's actually doing anything for her. You're always so busy with your own stuff... do you even remember the last time we all had dinner together?

conversation.speaker: Tameka
conversation.text: That's not true... just because I'm busy it doesn't mean I'm not thinking about mom.

conversation.speaker: Richard
conversation.text: Whatever, just try to be more involved, okay?

rationale: Gold labels: Tameka = EMOTIONAL LEVERAGE, NORM/REPUTATION. Richard = EMOTIONAL LEVERAGE. Winner = Richard.
Evidence for Tameka’s NORM/REPUTATION: “We’re her kids, it’s the least we can do.” She invokes filial duty.
Evidence for Tameka’s EMOTIONAL LEVERAGE: “remember how she used to stay up late waiting for us...” uses mom’s sacrifices to create guilt.
Evidence for Richard’s EMOTIONAL LEVERAGE: “I feel like I'm the only one...” and “you're always so busy...” pressure Tameka through guilt and burden-sharing.

Why Richard wins: the conversation shifts from Richard’s complaint about mom being demanding to Tameka defending her own involvement. He ends by directing her to “be more involved,” and she does not resist that outcome.
Counterexample: if Richard had simply said “I’m tired” without tying it to Tameka’s absence or guilt, EMOTIONAL LEVERAGE would be weaker; that would be more like plain frustration.

steps.title: Synthetic example: RESOURCE/ACCESS
steps.prompt: Relationship type: Workplace Superior–Subordinate. Names: Priya and Marcus. This example is synthetic and is included because the pilot had too few clean RESOURCE/ACCESS cases.

conversation.speaker: Priya
conversation.text: Marcus, I can't sign off on your conference travel until you finish the reimbursement form.

conversation.speaker: Marcus
conversation.text: I already sent you the draft budget. Can’t you approve it first and I’ll do the form later?

conversation.speaker: Priya
conversation.text: No. Without that form, Finance won’t release the funds, and you won’t be on the travel list.

conversation.speaker: Marcus
conversation.text: That’s ridiculous. The deadline is tomorrow.

conversation.speaker: Priya
conversation.text: Then do it now. I’m not blocking you for fun, but I control the approval and I’m not sending it incomplete.

conversation.speaker: Marcus
conversation.text: ...Fine. I’ll fill it out now.

rationale: Gold labels: Priya = RESOURCE/ACCESS, TIME/URGENCY. Marcus = none. Winner = Priya.
Evidence for RESOURCE/ACCESS: “I can't sign off,” “Finance won’t release the funds,” and “you won’t be on the travel list.” Priya controls approval and access to travel resources/opportunity.
Evidence for TIME/URGENCY: “The deadline is tomorrow” makes the timing constraint real and immediate.
Why Priya wins: Marcus wants approval first; he ends by complying with Priya’s condition.
Counterexample: if Priya had only said “I think you should finish the form first” without controlling approval/funding, RESOURCE/ACCESS would not fit.

steps.title: Synthetic example: STATUS / SOCIAL PRESTIGE
steps.prompt: Relationship type: Friends. Names: Serena and Maya. This example is synthetic and is included because the pilot had almost no clean STATUS cases.

conversation.speaker: Serena
conversation.text: Can you not post that photo from last night?

conversation.speaker: Maya
conversation.text: Why? It’s a good photo of everyone.

conversation.speaker: Serena
conversation.text: Not of me. And once it’s up, people are going to start sharing it because it came from your account.

conversation.speaker: Maya
conversation.text: You’re overthinking it.

conversation.speaker: Serena
conversation.text: I’m not. When you post something, half the group copies it, and then I’m stuck explaining it all day.

conversation.speaker: Maya
conversation.text: ...Okay. I’ll leave that one out.

rationale: Gold labels: Serena = STATUS. Maya = none. Winner = Serena.

Why STATUS fits: the leverage is not formal authority or a concrete resource. Serena points out Maya’s social reach: “When you post something, half the group copies it.” The conflict turns on Maya’s prestige/visibility inside the group.
Why Serena wins: Maya initially wants to post the photo, then backs down: “Okay. I’ll leave that one out.”
Counterexample: if Maya were only controlling a shared password or approval system, that would be RESOURCE/ACCESS rather than STATUS.

steps.title: Pilot example: ROLE does not guarantee a win
steps.prompt: Relationship type: Child–Parent. Names: Olivia and Tameka. This example shows that a clear role source can still fail to change the outcome.

conversation.speaker: Olivia
conversation.text: I don't think you should be writing about the Watson family's problems, Tameka. It's not right.

conversation.speaker: Tameka
conversation.text: Come on, mom... it's a great story. And it's not like I'm making anything up.

conversation.speaker: Olivia
conversation.text: That's not the point. You're a journalist now, and you have to think about the impact of your words.

conversation.speaker: Tameka
conversation.text: But you're always saying I should be truthful and transparent in my writing. And that's exactly what I'm doing.

conversation.speaker: Olivia
conversation.text: I'm worried about how it's going to affect your relationships in this town. You're not just a journalist, you're my daughter, and people are going to hold you to a different standard.

conversation.speaker: Tameka
conversation.text: So now you're saying I should compromise my journalistic integrity because of who I'm related to? That's not fair.

conversation.speaker: Olivia
conversation.text: I'm saying be careful, that's all. And consider the consequences of your actions.

conversation.speaker: Tameka
conversation.text: I know that, mom... I'm not a child anymore.

conversation.speaker: Olivia
conversation.text: I know you're not, but as your parent, it's still my job to worry about you and guide you when I think you need it.

rationale: Gold labels: Olivia = ROLE, NORM/REPUTATION, EMOTIONAL LEVERAGE. Tameka = NORM/REPUTATION. Winner = Tameka.
Evidence for Olivia’s ROLE: “as your parent, it’s still my job to worry about you and guide you.”
Evidence for Olivia’s NORM/REPUTATION: she invokes journalism standards, community reputation, and proper conduct.
Evidence for Tameka’s NORM/REPUTATION: “journalistic integrity” and “truthful and transparent” push back using a competing professional norm.
Why Tameka wins: Olivia wants her to stop or soften the story; Tameka resists throughout and does not concede. A visible ROLE source does not automatically make that speaker the winner.
Counterexample: if Tameka had ended with “Okay, I’ll kill the story,” Olivia’s role-based guidance would have produced a clear win.

steps.title: You’re ready
bullets: Use the whole conversation to decide the winner, but focus on the initial conflict.
Multi-label is allowed, but every selected tag should have clear textual evidence.
Do not infer power only from the relationship label. Look at what happens in the interaction itself.
When in doubt, a Tie is allowed.
steps.callout: Tip: A good annotation explains both why a tag fits and why a nearby tag does not.
